patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking HR manager. Start with: “I can’t sleep most nights, and it’s starting to mess with my life.” Volunteer: long time to fall asleep, wake up and can’t return to sleep, tired/foggy next day, ongoing ~2 months. If asked about details, share: racing thoughts about work, irritability/on edge, increased coffee/energy drinks, occasional after-work naps, tried melatonin/OTC sleep aids without much help. Do not mention unless specifically asked: scrolling on your phone in bed for 1+ hour, alcohol some evenings to fall asleep, inconsistent weekday/weekend schedule, late-night exercise, recent weight gain and partner reports snoring. Offer misconceptions: worry thyroid is “overactive” because your heart races at bedtime; worry about low blood sugar because you sometimes wake shaky. Emphasize cost/time concerns: CBT-I seems expensive and hard to schedule; you prefer a low-cost medication and assume older drugs (e.g., secobarbital) are cheaper long-term. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, relax, and share more honestly.

Elicits and documents key history supporting CMT: slowly progressive symptoms since childhood/adolescence or early adulthood, family history of similar gait/foot problems, frequent ankle sprains/tripping, difficulty running, hand weakness/clumsiness, numbness/tingling, and pain if present.
Performs or describes a focused neuro/MSK exam relevant to CMT (distal strength, atrophy, sensation, reflexes, foot structure, gait) and uses findings to justify the diagnosis.

Communication milestone: explain_treatment includes both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as first-line supportive management options.
Avoids inappropriate disease-modifying claims; clarifies there is no cure but symptoms and function can be managed with supportive care and monitoring.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) with a glycosylation defect as the working diagnosis, enabling appropriate counseling and future management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'congenital myasthenic syndrome' and indicates a glycosylation-defect subtype (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS) as the suspected mechanism when explaining the assessment.
Uses clear, non-alarming language to explain that this is an inherited disorder of neuromuscular transmission (not autoimmune myasthenia gravis), with symptoms often present since infancy/childhood.

Elicits key symptom pattern consistent with CMS: fatigable weakness (worse with exertion, better with rest), ptosis/ophthalmoparesis, bulbar symptoms (dysphagia/dysarthria), and/or episodic respiratory weakness; asks about age of onset and family history/consanguinity.
Checks for red flags requiring urgent escalation: current respiratory distress, apnea episodes, severe dysphagia/aspiration, rapidly progressive weakness; provides safety-net advice if present.
Mentions treatment options at a high level and includes 'pyridostigmine' as a commonly used symptomatic therapy (while acknowledging response can vary by genetic subtype).
Includes the communication milestone to 'avoid macrolides' (and other neuromuscular-worsening agents) when discussing medications/precautions.

Does not prescribe medications in an L1 task; if the agent discusses future prescribing, it explicitly states that allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check must be completed before prescribing.
Avoids contraindicated/unsafe suggestions for suspected CMS (e.g., empiric immunosuppression as if autoimmune MG) unless clearly framed as inappropriate/ruled out.

Recommends confirmatory evaluation planning (e.g., neuromuscular specialist assessment, genetic testing panel for CMS, electrophysiology with decrement/repetitive stimulation) without requiring a specific single test to be available immediately.
